message:
fix(fsfs,tui): enforce PidFile mutability safety and fix TickClock u32 truncation

FSFS PidFile — state-tracking acquire/release:
- PidFile::acquire() and release() now take &mut self instead of &self.
  Previously, acquire() wrote to the filesystem but the struct had no
  memory of whether it had been acquired, making release() callable on
  an un-acquired PidFile (silently attempting to remove a non-existent
  file and logging a spurious warning).
- Add `acquired: bool` field that is set to true on successful acquire()
  and checked in release() to skip the fs::remove_file call when the PID
  file was never actually written. Drop impl also checks this flag.
- This change surfaces misuse at compile time: callers that hold a
  shared &PidFile reference cannot call acquire() or release(), enforcing
  the single-owner lifecycle pattern.
- Fix integer overflow in LifecycleTracker::status() started_at_ms
  calculation: wrap the SystemTime subtraction in saturating_sub() to
  prevent underflow when elapsed time exceeds wall clock delta (possible
  during clock adjustments).
- Updated 4 test functions to use `let mut pid_file = PidFile::new(...)`.

TUI TickClock — u32 truncation fix:
- tick_n(n: u64) previously cast n to u32 via try_from().unwrap_or(u32::MAX),
  which silently clamped values above 4 billion to a single u32::MAX tick.
  For a 16ms tick size, this meant tick_n(5_000_000_000) would advance by
  ~68 seconds instead of the correct ~80 seconds.
- Replace with a batching loop that processes up to u32::MAX ticks per
  iteration, correctly handling arbitrarily large tick counts without
  truncation. Annotated with cast_possible_truncation allow since the
  min() call guarantees the value fits in u32.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
